ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Chinese cabbage and minced pork soup. Closest defensible anchor is pork soup food product (FOODON_03311737). Chinese cabbage component not individually coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (soups and broths, meat-based) is the closest defensible category for this pork-and-vegetable broth dish. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Vietnamese-style Chinese cabbage and minced pork soup.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific dish. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular codes for Vietnamese or Singaporean ethnic soup dishes.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Chinese cabbage and minced pork soup is approximately 250 g, comprising broth, softened cabbage leaves, and seasoned minced pork balls or loose mince.

nutrient_source.title: Chinese Cabbage (Minced Pork) — Canh Cai Thao Thit Bam
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) — Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database record for this dish (cr_id G00010550). All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not separately reported by HPB for this entry; value estimated from USDA FoodData Central ground pork reference (FDC ID 168351) proportionally adjusted for dish composition.

gi_source.title: N/A — negligible carbohydrate content; GI not applicable
gi_source.source_match_notes: Carbohydrate is 0.0 g/100g per HPB source. Glycemic index is not applicable as this dish contains negligible carbohydrates. Per the zero-carbohydrate GI rule, gi_category is set to not_applicable and glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0. The numeric value 0 is used here as a sentinel consistent with the template default for not-applicable GI foods with zero carbohydrate; this is not a measured GI value. No GI testing is required or meaningful for this food.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, reduce fish sauce and salt in the broth substantially and limit portion to no more than 100 g, as a single 250 g bowl delivers approximately 2625 mg sodium — more than the entire recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar per 100g; high protein (19.1 g/100g) supports glycemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes as it contains no carbohydrate or sugar and provides high-quality protein from minced pork, which supports satiety and glycemic control; monitor total meal carbohydrate from accompanying rice or noodles.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg/100g and cholesterol 89 mg/100g; saturated fat from minced pork contributes to cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: People with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish due to very high sodium content (1050 mg/100g) and moderate cholesterol (89 mg/100g); if consumed, use lean pork mince, reduce fish sauce and salt, and keep portion to a small serving alongside high-potassium vegetables to partially offset sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sodium (1050 mg/100g) worsens tissue swelling; pork-derived arachidonic acid amplifies inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should minimise this dish; reduce fish sauce and salt to lower sodium burden on inflamed joints, increase the proportion of Chinese cabbage to maximise cruciferous anti-inflammatory benefit, and choose leaner pork mince to reduce arachidonic acid intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Canh Cải Thảo Thịt Băm (Chinese Cabbage and Minced Pork Soup) is a light Vietnamese-style clear broth soup widely consumed in Singapore's Vietnamese and Chinese communities. It is prepared by simmering seasoned minced pork — shaped into loose balls or left as crumbled mince — with napa cabbage (Chinese cabbage) in a clear broth seasoned with fish sauce, salt, and garlic. The result is a savoury, mildly umami soup with tender cabbage leaves and juicy pork mince. The dish is nutritionally dense in protein (19.1 g/100g) and fat (10.5 g/100g) from the pork, while the cabbage contributes minimal carbohydrate and fibre. Sodium content is very high at 1050 mg/100g due to fish sauce and salt in the broth, making it unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease without significant recipe modification. Carbohydrate content is 0 g/100g, making it glycemically neutral and suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments. The dish is typically served as part of a shared Vietnamese or Singaporean family meal alongside steamed rice, and is common at lunch and dinner. It is not halal due to pork content. Chinese cabbage provides some calcium (19 mg/100g) and iron (1.14 mg/100g). The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (cr_id G00010550, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central ground pork reference (FDC ID 168351) as HPB does not separately report this field for this entry.
notes: carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Food Composition Database — this dish as prepared contains no starchy ingredients; the pork and cabbage combination yields negligible carbohydrate. sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB source. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source, which may reflect the broth-dominant composition and limited cabbage fibre in the analysed sample. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions not reported by HPB for this dish entry and not available in a directly matching approved source record. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data not reported by HPB for this dish; no directly matching approved-source record available as of 2026-04-16. Glycemic index is not applicable as this dish contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0 g/100g per HPB); gi_category set to not_applicable; glycemic_index field set to numeric 0 as template sentinel value — this is not a measured GI value but reflects the not-applicable state for a zero-carbohydrate food. Saturated fat (3.6 g/100g) estimated from USDA FoodData Central Ground pork, raw (FDC ID 168351) proportionally adjusted for dish composition — documented as adapted match. The dish contains pork and is not halal. Fish sauce is a fish allergen. The original input name contained a typographic character '麦' which appears to be a data-entry artefact; canonical name corrected to 'Chinese Cabbage and Minced Pork Soup (Canh Cải Thảo Thịt Băm)'.

veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Record passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8. Core macros sourced from HPB Singapore (approved primary source, confidence 0.95). Zero carbohydrate confirmed and justified. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central and documented. GI set to numeric 0 as sentinel for not-applicable state (zero-carbohydrate food), with gi_category 'not_applicable' and full source documentation. Ontology uses defensible closest-parent anchors with honest gap notes. Multilingual coverage satisfies Singaporean dish requirements (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) plus Vietnamese as the dish's origin language. All three required health conditions present with clinical specificity. Aliases consistent between flat array and structured object. LLM description exceeds 200 characters with full clinical and cultural context.
